demographics.trauma_type: Accidentally caused serious injury to another

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I detach myself from the memories
I worry obsessively about bad things happening to people I care about

Threat.value: Even when others tell me I am safe I find it impossible to believe it
I feel unsafe unless my back is against a wall where I can see the room

Memory.value: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
I feel like I am only remembering parts of what happened and the rest is missing
I cannot put the memories of the event in the right order

Negative Appraisals.value: The event happened to me because of the sort of person I am
The person I have become is unrecognisable to me
I can't deal with even the slightest upset

vignette:
Aung, a 54-year-old South Eastern Asian urban planner who is divorced, presents with persistent post-traumatic stress symptoms after a road incident in which he accidentally caused serious injury to another person. He describes the peak moment as the sudden impact, the person on the ground, and his own failure to react quickly enough to help in the way he believes he should have. In his mind, that single scene has become the point at which he decided he was “the sort of person this kind of thing happens through.”

When Aung speaks about the event, he does not tell it in a smooth sequence. He stops, circles back, and describes only fragments: the sound, the body falling, a flash of headlights, and then nothing clear in between. At times he says it feels as if the frightening scene is happening right now, not years ago. He struggles to place the pieces in order, and the gaps themselves seem to unsettle him more than the details he can recall.

Aung’s central stuck point is that the accident proved he is fundamentally at fault, and that the person he has become is no longer recognisable to him. He speaks in a close paraphrase of, “This happened because of the kind of man I am. I can’t handle even the smallest upset, so I am not who I thought I was.”

In daily life, Aung is hyper-alert to danger. Even when colleagues reassure him that he is safe, he says he cannot believe it. At work, in meetings at the planning office, he prefers to sit with his back against a wall so he can watch the whole room. He has difficulty concentrating on reports and drawings, his attention repeatedly pulled toward possible harm to people he cares about. He has also been avoiding starting new friendships or relationships, and since his divorce he has kept social contact limited and controlled.

The dominant pattern is self-reinforcing. His harsh appraisals feed his sense that the world is unsafe and that he cannot cope, which makes the accident feel more immediate and more fragmented in memory. That disorganised recollection then strengthens his belief that something in him is defective or blameworthy. His fear response keeps him scanning rooms, doubting reassurance, and holding himself at a distance from others, and his detachment from the memory leaves him with even less access to a settled account of what happened. In practice, this affects his work relationships: in open-plan project meetings he sits rigidly at the wall, misses parts of discussion, and withdraws from informal lunches, reinforcing his isolation.
